title: -Resolution in Support of California Senate Bill 81 (Arreguín) Health and Care Facilities: Information Sharing to Amend Sections 56.05 and 56.10 of the Civil Code, and to Add Chapter 1.5 to Division 2 of the Health and Safety Code, Relating to Health and Care Facilities

sections.recommendation:
Adopt a resolution in support of California State Senate (SB) Bill 81 (Arreguín),
Healthcare Provider Entities: Information Sharing amend Sections 56.05 and 56.10 of
the Civil Code, and to add Chapter 1.5 (commencing with Section 1249) to Division 2 of
the Health and Safety Code, relating to health and care facilities, and declaring the
urgency thereof, to take effect immediately; and distribute copies to the California
Governor Gavin Newsom, Senator Jesse Arreguín, Assemblymember Buffy Wicks,
California Senate President pro Tempore Mike McGuire, and California Assembly
Speaker Robert Rivas to advocate for its passage.
SB 81 would mandate healthcare facilities to develop protocols for monitoring and
receiving visitors, as well as designating certain areas as nonpublic in order to maintain
a secure environment aligned with the facility’s mission and commitment to patient care.
Additionally, the bill would prohibit immigration enforcement agents from entering
nonpublic areas of a facility without a valid judicial warrant or court order. It would also
protect patient information by barring the disclosure of a person’s current or past
immigration status or place of birth for purposes related to immigration enforcement.1
1 https://leginfo.legislature.ca.gov/faces/billTextClient.xhtml?bill_id=202520260SB81
Page 2 of 7
sections.current_situation:
In January of 2025, the City of Berkeley reaffirmed its status as a Sanctuary City, which
outlines that “no department, agency, commission, officer or employee of the City of
Berkeley shall use any City funds or resources to assist in the enforcement of Federal
immigration law or to gather or disseminate information on the status of individuals in
the City of Berkeley unless required by federal law.”2
Similarly, the California Values Act (SB 54) ensures that no state and local resources
are used to assist federal immigration enforcement and that schools, hospitals, and
courthouses are safe spaces for everyone.
SB 54 required the Attorney General to prepare and publish guidance promoting safe
and secure healthcare access for all in the state of California. This guidance is to
provide the administrators of healthcare provider entities with policies for collecting
patient and family health information, while preventing unnecessary collection and
maintenance of information on the immigration status of patients and their families.
SB 81 would require health and care facilities to restrict access for immigration
enforcement to its nonpublic areas without a valid judicial warrant or court order and
prohibit such facilities from disclosing current and previous immigration status or
birthplace as part of a patient’s medical information for the purposes of immigration
enforcement by amending Sections 56.05 and 56.10 of the Civil Code, and to add
Chapter 1.5 (commencing with Section 1249) to Division 2 of the Health and Safety
Code, Relating to Health and Care Facilities.
sections.fiscal_impacts:
Limited staff time associated with communicating the city’s position to the above-
referenced recipients.
sections.environmental:
There are no identifiable environmental effects or opportunities associated with the
subject of this report.

RESOLUTION NO. ##,###N.S.
RESOLUTION IN SUPPORT OF CALIFORNIA SENATE BILL 81 (ARREGUÍN)
CALIFORNIA FAIR ELECTIONS ACT OF 2026 TO AMEND SECTIONS 85300 AND
85320 OF THE GOVERNMENT CODE, RELATING TO THE POLITICAL REFORM
ACT OF 1974
WHEREAS, Senate Bill 81 (Arreguín) would require health and care facilities to restrict
access for immigration enforcement to its nonpublic areas without a valid judicial
warrant or court order and prohibit such facilities from disclosing current and previous
immigration status or birthplace as part of a patient’s medical information for the
purposes of immigration enforcement; and
WHEREAS, Senate Bill 81 is co-sponsored by the Service Employees International
Union (SEIU), California Immigrant Policy Center, Latino Coalition for a Healthy
California, and California Nurses Association, and supported by a broad coalition of
labor unions, immigrant rights organizations, not-for-profit community health centers,
civic engagement non-profits, civil rights advocates, healthcare policy advocates,
organizations for ethnic equity, and youth health commissions, who recognize it as a
critical step in protecting the civil rights of immigrants; and
WHEREAS, current law does not strictly prohibit health and care entities from sharing
patient birthplace and immigration status information with immigration enforcement,
leaving immigrants and their families vulnerable when accessing care that is essential to
life; and
WHEREAS, Senate Bill 81 seeks to explicitly prohibit patient birthplace and immigration
status information from being shared by healthcare entities to immigration enforcement
and restrict access for immigration enforcement to its nonpublic areas without a valid
judicial warrant or court order; and
WHEREAS, immigrants and their families have been targeted by the current federal
administration, increasing the immigrants’ dire need for support and protection; and
WHEREAS, the passage of Senate Bill 81 would he
